title: On the moral grammar of inheritance
summary: Examine the ethical logic of inheritance — what is owed to heirs, what is owed to others, and what the transfer of accumulated advantage says about the community that permits it.
topic: The ethics of inheritance and what it reveals about obligation, desert, and social arrangement
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay exploring the moral grammar of inheritance — the implicit rules about what one generation owes another, who deserves what they have not earned, and what the tolerance of large inheritance reveals about a society's conception of fairness. Do not write a policy essay for or against inheritance tax. Instead, treat inheritance as a philosophical problem: it forces a confrontation between the legitimacy of parental love, the arbitrariness of advantage, and the claims of the wider community. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid moralising at the reader. 5–7 paragraphs.
hintStarters: Rarely does the transfer of wealth between generations provoke the honest question it deserves, which is not whether inheritance is legal but…
What the defence of inheritance as an expression of parental love tends to obscure is…
Granted, the desire to provide for one's children is among the most powerful and least negotiable of human motivations. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: ngữ pháp đạo đức
lợi thế không kiếm được
sự tuỳ tiện của sự ra đời
sự chuyển giao liên thế hệ
đặc quyền tích luỹ
sự xứng đáng (về mặt đạo đức)
huyền thoại chế độ nhân tài
những đòi hỏi của cộng đồng
sự thiên vị của cha mẹ
nén lại cơ hội
khế ước công dân
lợi thế có tính cấu trúc
tự nhiên hoá bất bình đẳng
lòng hào phóng sau khi chết
người thừa kế không làm gì
may mắn về mặt đạo đức
nguyên tắc giá trị bình đẳng
củng cố, đào sâu thêm
để lại (tài sản) theo di chúc
dòng dõi, huyết thống

sampleText:
Rarely does the transfer of wealth between generations provoke the honest question it deserves, which is not whether inheritance is legal but whether the moral logic that justifies it can withstand scrutiny. The legal question has been settled, variously, across jurisdictions. The moral question has not — partly because to raise it is to interrogate not just the rich but every parent who has saved, renovated, or simply stayed in a house in an appreciating neighbourhood, intending to pass on what they have accumulated.

The usual defence of inheritance runs through parental love. Parents want to provide for their children; this desire is not merely permissible but admirable, a form of devotion that society has good reason to support rather than penalise. On this account, an inheritance is not a random windfall but the tangible residue of a parent's lifetime of care — something that belongs, in a morally serious sense, to the relationship rather than to the individual. To tax or prohibit it is to reach inside a family and extract what private sacrifice has produced.

What the defence of inheritance as an expression of parental love tends to obscure is the difference between giving and entrenching. A parent who reads to a child, who sacrifices time and income to provide better schooling, who passes on knowledge, confidence, and a sense of social ease, is doing something that is difficult to disentangle from love. But a parent who bequeaths a child three hundred thousand pounds in property is doing something structurally different: not transferring a relationship but compressing opportunity, making it more likely that the child will have more and that others, who happened to be born into families without accumulated assets, will have less. The act is private in its origin but structural in its effect.

Granted, the desire to provide for one's children is among the most powerful and least negotiable of human motivations. Even so, the appeal to parental love does not settle the moral question; it only restates it. Every privilege a parent confers on a child is, in some sense, a disadvantage conferred on another child who did not receive the same. Parental partiality is real and understandable; the question is how far it is permitted to extend before it undermines the civic compact that makes the wider society function — the implicit agreement that people's life chances should not be determined entirely by the accident of their lineage.

It is the tolerance of large inheritance, not the inheritance of modest amounts, that most clearly reveals a society's operative conception of desert. A society that permits one person to receive, by the fact of birth, more wealth than another will accumulate in a lifetime of labour has made a decision — not necessarily a conscious one — about what it believes people are owed independently of anything they have done. The meritocratic fiction — that advantage is earned and disadvantage is deserved — is most difficult to sustain when confronted with an heir who did nothing more to deserve their position than survive their parents. That confrontation is not a marginal case; in societies with significant inherited wealth, it is a common condition.

This is not an argument that inheritance should be abolished. It is an argument that the moral grammar of inheritance is more complex than its defenders usually acknowledge. The difficulty is not merely that some people inherit more than others — all societies involve unequal distributions of luck, including moral luck — but that inherited wealth, unlike other forms of luck, can be managed by policy and is sustained by legal structures that the community itself maintains. The question of what to do with that knowledge is a political one. But the prior question — whether what we have called the justice of inheritance is in fact anything of the sort — belongs to moral philosophy rather than to electoral arithmetic.

Some of what passes for a defence of inheritance is in practice a defence of the status quo — the observation that inheritance has always occurred and is difficult to disentangle from love and from legitimate savings. These are real difficulties. But the difficulty of addressing something is not a moral argument for tolerating it unchanged. The heir who inherits and the child who does not are not, at birth, different in any way that the moral philosophy of equal worth recognises as relevant. That gap, between what moral philosophy implies and what the legal system permits, is where the real question lives.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Rarely' to open with measured authority
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Rarely does the transfer of wealth between generations provoke the honest question it deserves' đảo trợ động từ 'does' trước chủ ngữ 'the transfer of wealth' sau 'Rarely'. Đây là đảo ngữ phủ định trang trọng, không phải 'never' mà 'rarely' — thừa nhận rằng câu hỏi này đôi khi được đặt ra nhưng không đủ thường xuyên. Độ chính xác này là quan trọng ở cấp C1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Rarely does the transfer of wealth between generations provoke the honest question it deserves' inverts 'does' before the subject 'the transfer' after the negative adverb 'Rarely'. The choice of 'rarely' rather than 'never' is deliberate: the claim is precise and hedged, not absolute — registering that the question is sometimes raised, just not with sufficient honesty or frequency.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft to focus the essay's sharpest claim
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the tolerance of large inheritance, not the inheritance of modest amounts, that most clearly reveals a society's operative conception of desert' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'It is X, not Y, that Z' để phân biệt giữa thừa kế khiêm tốn (có thể biện hộ được) và thừa kế lớn (cho thấy xã hội thực sự tin vào điều gì về sự xứng đáng). Cấu trúc này làm cho sự phân biệt sắc nét nhất của bài có tính cú pháp.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the tolerance of large inheritance, not the inheritance of modest amounts, that most clearly reveals a society's operative conception of desert' uses 'It is X, not Y, that Z' to draw the essay's sharpest distinction. The target is not modest family savings but the toleration of very large transfers — and the cleft isolates that target precisely.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hiếm khi sự chuyển giao tài sản giữa các thế hệ khơi dậy câu hỏi trung thực mà nó xứng đáng được nhận, không phải là liệu thừa kế có hợp pháp không mà liệu logic đạo đức biện hộ cho nó có thể chịu đựng được sự kiểm tra. Câu hỏi pháp lý đã được giải quyết. Câu hỏi đạo đức thì chưa.

Sự bảo vệ thông thường của thừa kế đi qua tình yêu cha mẹ. Cha mẹ muốn cung cấp cho con cái; mong muốn này không chỉ được phép mà còn đáng ngưỡng mộ. Đánh thuế hoặc cấm nó là tiếp cận bên trong một gia đình và lấy đi những gì sự hy sinh riêng tư đã tạo ra.

Những gì việc bảo vệ thừa kế như là biểu hiện của tình yêu cha mẹ thường che khuất là sự khác biệt giữa cho và củng cố. Nhưng cha mẹ để lại tài sản lớn đang làm điều gì đó khác về mặt cấu trúc: nén lại cơ hội, làm cho có nhiều khả năng hơn là đứa trẻ sẽ có nhiều hơn và những người khác sẽ có ít hơn. Hành động đó có nguồn gốc riêng tư nhưng có tác động mang tính cấu trúc.

Cứ cho là, mong muốn cung cấp cho con cái của mình là một trong những động lực mạnh mẽ nhất và ít có thể thương lượng nhất của con người. Dù vậy, sự kêu gọi đến tình yêu cha mẹ không giải quyết câu hỏi đạo đức; nó chỉ tái phát biểu câu hỏi đó. Câu hỏi là sự thiên vị cha mẹ được phép mở rộng đến đâu trước khi nó làm suy yếu khế ước công dân.

Chính sự khoan nhượng đối với thừa kế lớn, không phải thừa kế số lượng khiêm tốn, mới tiết lộ rõ nhất khái niệm vận hành của một xã hội về sự xứng đáng. Huyền thoại chế độ nhân tài khó duy trì nhất khi đối mặt với người thừa kế không làm gì hơn để xứng đáng với vị trí của họ ngoài việc sống sót sau khi cha mẹ qua đời.

Đây không phải là lập luận rằng thừa kế nên bị bãi bỏ. Đây là lập luận rằng ngữ pháp đạo đức của thừa kế phức tạp hơn những người bảo vệ nó thường thừa nhận. Tài sản thừa kế, không giống các hình thức may mắn khác, có thể được quản lý bằng chính sách và được duy trì bởi các cấu trúc pháp lý mà chính cộng đồng duy trì.

Sự khó khăn trong việc giải quyết điều gì đó không phải là lập luận đạo đức để chịu đựng nó mà không thay đổi. Khoảng cách đó, giữa những gì triết học đạo đức ngụ ý và những gì hệ thống pháp lý cho phép, là nơi câu hỏi thực sự tồn tại.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay identify as 'the honest question' that inheritance rarely provokes?
options: Whether inheritance should be subject to higher taxation
Whether the moral logic that justifies inheritance can withstand scrutiny
Whether parents should be allowed to give money to their children
Whether inheritance is more common now than in previous generations
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the honest question is 'not whether inheritance is legal but whether the moral logic that justifies it can withstand scrutiny.'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies inversion after 'Rarely'?
options: Rarely the transfer of wealth between generations provokes the honest question it deserves.
The transfer of wealth rarely provokes the honest question between generations.
Rarely does the transfer of wealth between generations provoke the honest question it deserves.
Rarely, one can argue, does the transfer of wealth provoke the kind of honest question.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Rarely', the auxiliary 'does' must precede the subject 'the transfer of wealth' — this is obligatory subject-auxiliary inversion following a negative adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: Negative adverbs at the start of a clause require subject-auxiliary inversion.

mcQuestions.prompt: What distinction does the essay draw between 'giving' and 'entrenching'?
options: Giving is a smaller act than entrenching, which is reserved for large sums
Giving transfers a relationship; entrenching compresses opportunity and creates structural advantage for one child at the expense of others
Giving is voluntary; entrenching is legally required
Giving is an act of love; entrenching is an act of power
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay contrasts acts like reading to a child (transferring a relationship) with bequeathing property (compressing opportunity, making structural differences more entrenched).
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the third paragraph for the distinction.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the cleft 'It is X, not Y, that Z'?
options: It is modest inheritance, not large amounts, that reveals a society's conception of desert.
What reveals a society's conception of desert is both small and large inheritance equally.
It is the tolerance of large inheritance, not the inheritance of modest amounts, that most clearly reveals a society's operative conception of desert.
Desert is revealed by inheritance, whether large or modest.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is the tolerance of large inheritance, not the inheritance of modest amounts, that most clearly reveals…' correctly uses the cleft to isolate the essay's key distinction.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is … not … that …' structure in the fifth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the meritocratic fiction' refer to?
options: The false belief that effort alone determines outcomes
The story that advantage is earned and disadvantage is deserved — which cannot be sustained when confronted with inherited wealth
A fictional narrative about social mobility used in political speeches
The belief that meritocracy is the best system for distributing rewards
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says: 'The meritocratic fiction — that advantage is earned and disadvantage is deserved — is most difficult to sustain when confronted with an heir who did nothing more to deserve their position than survive their parents.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fifth paragraph for the definition.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the 'Granted… Even so…' structure concede and counter?
options: Grants that inheritance is legal; counters that it is immoral
Grants that parental love is a powerful motivation; counters that this love does not settle the moral question
Grants that some societies tax inheritance; counters that others do not
Grants that wealthy heirs did nothing to deserve their position; counters that they cannot help their birth
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concedes that parental desire to provide is 'the most powerful and least negotiable of human motivations' but counters that 'the appeal to parental love does not settle the moral question; it only restates it.'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'moral luck' mean in the context of the essay?
options: The good fortune of being born into a moral family
The idea that some of people's advantages and disadvantages are due to factors outside their control — including birth circumstances
The luck that allows moral people to succeed in competitive situations
The random occurrence of moral dilemmas in daily life
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'moral luck' to refer to unchosen advantages and disadvantages — including the accident of birth. Inherited wealth is a form of moral luck that, unlike others, can be managed by policy.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at how the essay uses 'moral luck' in the sixth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'desert' mean as used in the phrase 'conception of desert'?
options: A dry, barren landscape
What a person deserves based on their actions or character
The final course of a meal
A mathematical concept related to distribution
mcQuestions.explanation: In moral philosophy, 'desert' (pronounced /dɪˈzɜːt/) refers to what a person morally deserves — their just reward or share. The essay asks what a society believes people are owed independently of what they have done.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a philosophical term — not the landscape or the sweet course of a meal.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the act is private in its origin but structural in its effect' mean?
options: Inheritance decisions are made privately but must be reported to public authorities
The private motivation (parental love) produces effects (inequality of opportunity) that operate at the structural level of society
Private companies can structure inheritance differently from private individuals
The structural complexity of inheritance law makes private arrangements difficult
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that while the act of inheritance springs from private motivation (love), its structural effect is to compress opportunity and make others' life chances worse — a consequence that operates at the societal level.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of the third paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the civic compact' refer to?
options: A formal agreement between citizens and their government about rights and duties
The implicit agreement that life chances should not be determined entirely by birth circumstances
A document signed by all citizens accepting the rules of inheritance
The compact between generations to care for each other
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'civic compact' to mean 'the implicit agreement that people's life chances should not be determined entirely by the accident of their lineage' — the social contract that large inheritance potentially undermines.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of the fourth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say the difficulty of addressing inheritance means, morally?
options: That policy reform should be postponed until public opinion shifts
That the difficulty is itself a moral argument for tolerating the status quo
That the difficulty of addressing it does not constitute a moral argument for tolerating it unchanged
That inheritance is too complex for moral philosophy and should be left to law
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly states: 'the difficulty of addressing something is not a moral argument for tolerating it unchanged' — refusing the common move of citing practical difficulty as a moral justification.

options: To make inequality feel like a natural and inevitable feature of the world, rather than a product of policy
To integrate immigrants into an unequal society
To study inequality using methods borrowed from the natural sciences
To reduce inequality through natural processes like competition
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To naturalise inequality' means to present it as normal, inevitable, and not subject to deliberate arrangement — the essay argues that defending inherited privilege on the grounds of love contributes to this naturalisation.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'naturalise' means: to make something seem natural and therefore beyond question.

options: The tendency of parents to judge their children's work too harshly
The preference parents feel for their own children over other children
The partial information parents have about their children's development
The legal right of parents to make partial financial transfers to their children
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Parental partiality' refers to the natural preference parents have for their own children — a form of bias that the essay acknowledges as 'real and understandable' while questioning how far it can justifiably extend.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about 'partiality' as a form of bias or preference.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about the 'defence of the status quo' in inheritance debates?
options: It is a valid philosophical position that deserves serious consideration
It often passes for moral argument but is really just an observation that inheritance has always occurred and is difficult to change
It is supported by the strongest philosophical arguments available
It should be accepted because most people agree with it
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'what passes for a defence of inheritance is in practice a defence of the status quo — the observation that inheritance has always occurred and is difficult to disentangle from love and from legitimate savings.'

options: A diplomatic exchange between representatives of different countries
The passing of wealth, property, or advantage from one generation to the next
The transfer of knowledge between younger and older employees in a workplace
A financial product designed to fund retirement across generations
mcQuestions.explanation: An 'intergenerational transfer' is the movement of wealth, assets, or advantages from one generation to the next — the essay examines the moral grammar governing when and to whom this is permissible.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Intergenerational' simply means between generations; 'transfer' means passing something from one person or group to another.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay distinguishes between transferring a relationship (reading to a child, building confidence) and transferring structural advantage (bequeathing property that compresses others' opportunity). But is this distinction as clean as the essay implies? Most parents would say that saving money for a child's education or housing is also an act of love inseparable from the relationship. At what point, if any, does parental provision become something the wider community has a legitimate interest in limiting?
